tagged_story_for_tts:
Eyes beyond human sight.<[silence:10ms]> Survival through unseen light.

"Animal vision adaptations" refers to how creatures see the world in ways humans can’t—using colors, heat, or motion invisible to us.

A chameleon’s eyes move independently, like two periscopes scanning for insects.<[silence:10ms]> One eye watches a bug while the other checks for predators.<[silence:10ms]> Their retinas have a double layer of cells: one for color, one for detecting fast movement—a built-in slow-motion camera.<[silence:10ms]> Eagles spot a rabbit from 1.5 miles away, their eyes packed with five times more light-sensing cells than ours.<[silence:10ms]> Mantis shrimp see twelve primary colors (we see three) and detect polarized light, turning water into a secret code of reflections.

Deep-sea fish use bioluminescent lures, glowing like fishing rods to attract prey in total darkness.<[silence:10ms]> Snakes "see" heat via pit organs, turning warm-blooded animals into glowing targets against cold backgrounds.

Three related subjects are color beyond rainbows, night vision mechanics, and how brains decode invisible signals.<[silence:10ms]>
clean_story:
Eyes beyond human sight. Survival through unseen light.

"Animal vision adaptations" refers to how creatures see the world in ways humans can’t—using colors, heat, or motion invisible to us.

A chameleon’s eyes move independently, like two periscopes scanning for insects. One eye watches a bug while the other checks for predators. Their retinas have a double layer of cells: one for color, one for detecting fast movement—a built-in slow-motion camera. Eagles spot a rabbit from 1.5 miles away, their eyes packed with five times more light-sensing cells than ours. Mantis shrimp see twelve primary colors (we see three) and detect polarized light, turning water into a secret code of reflections.

Deep-sea fish use bioluminescent lures, glowing like fishing rods to attract prey in total darkness. Snakes "see" heat via pit organs, turning warm-blooded animals into glowing targets against cold backgrounds.

Three related subjects are color beyond rainbows, night vision mechanics, and how brains decode invisible signals.
